name: Negation: 不 and 没
summary: Chinese has two words for 'not'. Learn when to use 不 (bù) versus 没 (méi).
explanation:
Mandarin negates with two different words. Use 不 (bù) for the present and future, for habits, and for opinions or wishes: 我不喝咖啡 (wǒ bù hē kāfēi) = I don't drink coffee.

Use 没 (méi) to negate the verb 有 (yǒu, to have) and to say an action did not happen in the past: 我没有时间 (wǒ méiyǒu shíjiān) = I don't have time; 我没去 (wǒ méi qù) = I didn't go.

A useful rule of thumb: 不 negates the present and future, while 没 negates the past and 有. The tone of 不 also changes to bú before a fourth tone, as in 不是 (bú shì).
pattern: 不 + verb (present/future)  |  没(有) + verb (past) / 没有 (not have)
tones: 不 is bù, but becomes bú before a fourth-tone syllable (不是 = bú shì).

examples.en: She is not a teacher. (bú shì)
I haven't seen that film yet.

commonMistakes: Using 不 with 有: it is 没有 (méiyǒu), never 不有.
Using 不 for the past: a past action that did not happen takes 没, as in 我没去.
Keeping 了 with 没: a negated past drops 了, so it is 我没吃, not 我没吃了.
Forgetting the tone change of 不 to bú before a fourth tone (不是 = bú shì).
Placing the negation after the verb. 不 and 没 always go before the verb.
tip: Two-word memory hook: 不 for now and later, 没 for the past and for 有. Master that split and negation is solved.
